personaLabel: Liberty STEM Charter School (Demo Charter School)

consultant.executiveSummary: Liberty STEM Charter School projects $18,125,509 in Year 5 revenue with a 69.3% net margin. There is 1 area to strengthen, and the recommendations below will help you build a more compelling financial story.
consultant.biggestRisk: Public funding is the primary revenue source — model disbursement timing carefully and maintain cash reserves for funding gaps

recommendations.title: Charter Funding Timing & Cash Flow Risk
recommendations.description: 94.7% of revenue comes from public per-pupil funding — this is enrollment-driven income and a strong foundation. The primary risk is disbursement timing: charter funding follows a state-defined schedule, so ensure you have cash reserves or a line of credit to cover gaps between enrollment counts and payment receipt.

recommendations.title: Stress-Test Your Enrollment Assumptions
recommendations.description: Model what happens if enrollment comes in 20% below plan. Understanding your downside scenario helps you prepare contingency plans.

recommendations.title: Document Your Growth Strategy
recommendations.description: Great projections are backed by a clear plan. Document your marketing strategy, enrollment pipeline, and community outreach, as this is the roadmap that turns your numbers into reality.

consultant.lenderReadinessExplanation: Your model has real promise, and there are just a few areas to strengthen. The recommendations above will help you build a more resilient financial plan, and every improvement makes your projections more achievable.

capTier.rationale: Below 30% evidence tagging, lenders in Lending Lab Cycle 1 bounced 6 of 7 packets with a 'show your work' response before opening any underwriting conversation. The model has not demonstrated assumption rigor to a lender-credible threshold.
capTier.source: Lending Lab Cycle 1 outcomes (Jan–Apr 2026, 18 packets, 3 lenders, 3 personas) — see ./lender-readiness-caps.calibration.md

keyMetrics.name: Revenue per Student (Year 1)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Healthy per-student revenue provides a solid foundation for sustainability.

keyMetrics.name: Cost per Student (Year 1)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Total cost per student is $14,082, well within revenue per student of $24,375, leaving a healthy margin.

keyMetrics.name: Staffing Cost (% of Revenue)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Staffing costs are well-controlled, giving you room for other priorities.

keyMetrics.name: Operating Cost (% of Revenue)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Operating costs are lean relative to revenue.

keyMetrics.name: Net margin (Year 1)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Year 1 shows a healthy surplus, a strong start for a new school.

keyMetrics.name: Net margin (Year 5)
keyMetrics.interpretation: By Year 5 the model shows strong net income, and your school is on track for financial sustainability.

keyMetrics.name: 5-Year Revenue Growth
keyMetrics.interpretation: Strong projected revenue growth over the 5-year period.

keyMetrics.name: Capacity Utilization (Year 5)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Year 5 enrollment approaches facility capacity, an efficient use of space.

keyMetrics.name: Days Cash on Hand (Year 1)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Cash reserves cover 90+ days of expenses — a strong liquidity position.

keyMetrics.name: Cash Trough (Minimum Cash Month)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Your lowest cash point is Month 1 at $200,000. You have enough reserves to weather the collection gap — this is what lenders want to see.
keyMetrics.benchmark: Target: Positive cash balance every month

keyMetrics.name: Debt Service Coverage Ratio (Year 1)
keyMetrics.interpretation: DSCR is above 1.25x, and your operating income comfortably covers debt payments.

keyMetrics.name: Public Funding (% of Revenue)
keyMetrics.interpretation: The model is heavily dependent on public funding, so develop contingency plans for policy changes.

keyMetrics.name: Operating Reserve (Year 5)
keyMetrics.interpretation: By Year 5, the school has built a healthy operating reserve of 3+ months, a strong foundation for long-term stability.

enrollmentGuidance: Year 1 to Year 2 projects 50% enrollment growth. Growth over 25% in a single year is uncommon and may require aggressive marketing or facility expansion.
Year 2 to Year 3 projects 33% enrollment growth. Growth over 25% in a single year is uncommon and may require aggressive marketing or facility expansion.

Strong demand signal: 365 applications + waitlist entries cover 183% of Year 1 projected enrollment (200 seats). Your projections are backed by real demand, which is exactly the kind of evidence that makes a model credible.

topIssues.title: Let's extend your cash runway
topIssues.summary: Based on your projected revenue and expenses, cash currently lasts 11.1 months. Aiming for 18+ months gives you room to absorb late tuition payments or delayed public funding without scrambling.
topIssues.whyItMatters: An 18-month runway means a single late tuition cycle or delayed state disbursement isn't an emergency. Building the cushion now is far easier than rebuilding it after opening day.
topIssues.recommendedAction: Build a cash buffer through startup fundraising, a line of credit, or by reducing early expenses. Target at least 18 months of runway before opening.
topIssues.nextStep: Open Step 2: School Details and raise opening cash, or trim Year 1 expenses in Step 7, until projected cash runway clears 18 months.

topIssues.title: Let's back the enrollment ramp with evidence
topIssues.summary: Year 1 to Year 2 projects 50% enrollment growth. Adding documented demand (waitlists, signed letters, or a clear recruitment pipeline) makes this story land.
topIssues.whyItMatters: Enrollment is the engine of every revenue and staffing assumption downstream. The more evidence we can attach to each year's number, the more confident the rest of the model becomes.
topIssues.recommendedAction: Back every enrollment target with documented demand — signed letters of intent, waitlist depth, community survey results, or recruitment pipeline data. Growth over 25% per year typically requires an exceptional recruitment engine or facility expansion.
topIssues.nextStep: Open Step 4: Enrollment and either soften the steepest year to a 15-25% jump, or paste your waitlist count, signed letters of intent, and recruitment plan into the Story step so reviewers see the evidence.

topIssues.title: Let's tie new hires to enrollment milestones
topIssues.summary: In Year 2, staffing cost growth runs more than 10 points ahead of enrollment growth. Sequencing hires to match enrollment keeps the model resilient.
topIssues.whyItMatters: Hiring slightly behind enrollment, rather than ahead of it, keeps fixed costs aligned with the revenue actually arriving. It also gives you a clean answer to 'what triggers each new role'.
topIssues.recommendedAction: Align staffing additions to enrollment milestones. Hire when students are enrolled, not when you hope they will be. Use part-time or contract roles to bridge gaps.
topIssues.nextStep: Open Step 6: Staffing and tie each new role's start date to a specific enrollment milestone (e.g. 'add when Y2 enrollment hits 60'), or push the role into a later year.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: The model reaches net income early and sustains strong margins through Year 5.
healthSignals.watchItem: Compare your real Year 1 enrollment against this projection as students enroll.
healthSignals.nextStep: Re-check this dimension after every enrollment or major staffing change in Steps 4–6.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: Cash stays positive throughout the projection and reserves exceed 3 months of expenses.
healthSignals.watchItem: Maintain reserves as a buffer against seasonal revenue dips.
healthSignals.nextStep: Keep cash and reserves where they are; revisit Step 2: School Details after any large capital plan change.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: Every month generates at least as much cash as it consumes — no timing gaps to bridge.
healthSignals.watchItem: Re-check after any change to billing cadence or payroll timing in Step 6.
healthSignals.nextStep: Re-check this dimension after every billing-cadence or payroll-timing change in Steps 5-6.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: Staffing at 35% of revenue leaves room for facilities, programs, and reserves.
healthSignals.watchItem: As you add staff, ensure staffing stays below 60% of revenue.
healthSignals.nextStep: Re-check after every staffing change in Step 6 to make sure payroll stays under 60% of revenue.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: Facility costs at 5% of revenue are well-managed and leave budget for programs.
healthSignals.watchItem: Watch for rent escalation clauses that could push this higher over time.
healthSignals.nextStep: Watch your lease for rent escalation clauses and re-check this signal in Step 7 each year.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: DSCR of 30.64x provides comfortable coverage of debt payments with room to spare.
healthSignals.watchItem: Maintain this ratio as you take on any additional debt.
healthSignals.nextStep: Re-check DSCR after any new debt is added in Step 5 to keep coverage at or above 1.25x.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: 95% of revenue comes from public funding. This is typical for charter schools, but disbursement timing and policy changes can create cash flow gaps.
healthSignals.watchItem: Model disbursement timing carefully, maintain cash reserves for funding gaps, and track legislative changes that could affect per-pupil allocations.
healthSignals.nextStep: Open Step 5: Revenue and grow tuition or per-pupil lines, or document grant renewal evidence in Step 1: Story.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: 267 days of cash on hand exceeds the 90-day benchmark, providing a strong liquidity cushion.
healthSignals.watchItem: Maintain this buffer to weather seasonal revenue dips or unexpected expenses.
healthSignals.nextStep: Maintain this buffer; re-check after any large planned outflow.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: 77.7 months of reserves by Year 5 — a strong buffer for unexpected costs.
healthSignals.watchItem: Continue growing reserves toward 6 months for best-in-class financial health.
healthSignals.nextStep: Keep building toward 6 months of reserves through controlled spending in Step 7.

criteria.name: Leader Compensation
criteria.detail: School leader draws a salary. This is a fundamental requirement for sustainable operations.

criteria.name: Philanthropy Dependency
criteria.threshold: ≤25% in every year, decreasing over time
criteria.detail: Fundraising is healthy and encouraged. But your school must be able to keep the lights on without it. At full enrollment, your budget should balance on earned revenue alone. Grants should fund growth and mission extras, not basic operations.

criteria.name: Personnel Cost Ratio
criteria.detail: Personnel costs exceed 60% of total expenses. This leaves too little room for facilities, curriculum, and contingency. A well-run school allocates no more than 60 cents of every dollar spent to people.

criteria.name: Facility Cost Ratio
criteria.detail: Facility costs are well within the target range, leaving healthy budget room for personnel and programming.

criteria.name: Debt Service Coverage
criteria.detail: DSCR of 30.64x provides strong debt service coverage with healthy cushion across all years.

criteria.name: Current Ratio
criteria.detail: No accounts payable or current debt reported, so the current ratio cannot be evaluated. If your school has any bills due within 12 months, enter them under Opening Balances.

criteria.name: Net Margin Trajectory
criteria.detail: Net margin trajectory is healthy, reaching break-even by Year 3 and growing to a sustainable level.

criteria.name: Minimum Enrollment
criteria.detail: Year 1 enrollment of 200 students meets the minimum viability threshold.

criteria.name: Revenue Per Pupil
criteria.detail: Revenue per pupil of $24,375 meets the minimum threshold for sustainable operations.

lendingLabAssessment.summary: 1 area needs attention before applying. Most founders need 2–3 iterations to get here. Adjust your model and the assessment updates automatically.

assumptionFlags.defaultPrompt: You're projecting 50% enrollment growth from Year 1 to Year 2. What's driving this — a new grade level, second location, or marketing push? Lenders will want specifics.
assumptionFlags.nextStep: Open Step 4: Enrollment and either soften the steepest year to a 15-25% jump or document the new grade, second site, or marketing push driving the growth in Step 1: Story.

assumptionFlags.defaultPrompt: Year 1 enrollment fills only 33% of your building capacity. Is this a phased growth strategy, or could you open with more students?
assumptionFlags.nextStep: Open Step 4: Enrollment and either grow Year 1 to fill at least 50% of capacity, or note the phased-growth strategy in Step 1: Story.

assumptionFlags.currentValue: Y1 revenue per student of $24,375 is above the typical AZ charter range. Verify per-pupil rate breakdown.
assumptionFlags.benchmark: $9,000–$15,000 typical, $18,000 ceiling (internal estimate, calibration pending (AZ DOE Equalization Assistance Schedule))
assumptionFlags.defaultPrompt: Year 1 revenue per student computes to $24,375, above the $18,000 ceiling for AZ charter schools (internal estimate, calibration pending (AZ DOE Equalization Assistance Schedule); typical range $9,000–$15,000). Either confirm the per-pupil rate breakdown lender-side or trim Step 5: Revenue lines that are stacking on the same seat.
assumptionFlags.nextStep: Open Step 5: Revenue and confirm each per-pupil rate against internal estimate, calibration pending (AZ DOE Equalization Assistance Schedule); if the stack is intentional, capture the rate-build narrative in Step 1: Story.

scenarios.name: Enrollment -10%
scenarios.description: All five enrollment years scaled down by 10% — the standard lender enrollment-miss sensitivity.

scenarios.name: Enrollment -20%
scenarios.description: All five enrollment years scaled down by 20% — the stretch downside lenders use to confirm the school can survive a soft-launch.

scenarios.name: Public Funding Delayed 3 Months (public sources only)
scenarios.description: Year-1 ESA, school-choice, and public-funding receipts reduced by 25% (≈ 3 months delayed) to simulate a slow first disbursement.

scenarios.name: Rent Shock +25%
scenarios.description: Occupancy & facility expense rows escalated by 25% — covers a lease renewal, market-rate reset, or unbudgeted CAM/utilities surprise.

scenarios.name: Founder Comp Normalized to Market
scenarios.description: Founder compensation re-cast at the market-rate benchmark (with benefits + payroll tax). The lender / board view of true operating cost.

narrative.headline: This model shows promise but has areas that should be strengthened before presenting to lenders.
narrative.summary: Liberty STEM Charter School projects $18,125,509 in Year 5 revenue with a 69.3% net margin. There is 1 area to strengthen, and the recommendations below will help you build a more compelling financial story. 0 of 9 health dimensions are healthy, while 9 need attention. Cash runway extends 11.1 months before additional funding would be needed.
keyRisks: Public funding is the primary revenue source — model disbursement timing carefully and maintain cash reserves for funding gaps

Let's extend your cash runway: Based on your projected revenue and expenses, cash currently lasts 11.1 months. Aiming for 18+ months gives you room to absorb late tuition payments or delayed public funding without scrambling.

Let's back the enrollment ramp with evidence: Year 1 to Year 2 projects 50% enrollment growth. Adding documented demand (waitlists, signed letters, or a clear recruitment pipeline) makes this story land.
Let's tie new hires to enrollment milestones: In Year 2, staffing cost growth runs more than 10 points ahead of enrollment growth. Sequencing hires to match enrollment keeps the model resilient.
Viability: The model reaches net income early and sustains strong margins through Year 5.
keyStrengths: Revenue per Student (Year 1): $24,375 — Healthy per-student revenue provides a solid foundation for sustainability.
Cost per Student (Year 1): $14,082 — Total cost per student is $14,082, well within revenue per student of $24,375, leaving a healthy margin.
Staffing Cost (% of Revenue): 35.3% — Staffing costs are well-controlled, giving you room for other priorities.
Operating Cost (% of Revenue): 22.2% — Operating costs are lean relative to revenue.
narrative.recommendedFocus: Continue refining the model and monitoring key metrics as the school develops.

sections.title: School Overview
sections.narrative: Liberty STEM Charter School is a Startup / Pre-Opening Charter School organized as a 501(c)(3) Nonprofit with a Public Funded (Charter) funding profile.

sections.title: Enrollment Plan
sections.narrative: Enrollment starts at 200 students in Year 1 and grows to 600 by Year 5 (200% growth).

sections.title: Revenue Model
sections.narrative: The revenue model is primarily publicly funded (94.7% of Year 1 revenue). Year 1 total revenue is projected at $4.9M, growing to $18.1M by Year 5.

rows.label: Residual reconciliation (collection rate, delinquency, scholarship treatment, other engine adjustments)

tables.title: Revenue Quality (Contracted vs. Projected vs. Donor / Policy Dependent)

rows.label: Hard revenue coverage of fixed + debt

insights.label: Risk framing — policy-dependent revenue
insights.body: 95% of Year 1 revenue comes from state per-pupil (ADM) funding (AZ), which the model classifies as policy-dependent. That bucket is excluded from the hard-revenue numerator by design — it produces the conservative Hard Revenue Coverage (Y1) of 0.00× shown above. Per-pupil and voucher dollars ARE contractual once a child is enrolled and eligibility is verified; the conservative classification reflects legislative / court risk to the funding program itself, not to individual student revenue. Reviewers underwriting against enrollment-backed coverage should look at the per-program break-even and the enrollment-risk stress tests in addition to the hard-revenue ratio.

sections.title: Staffing Plan
sections.narrative:
Staffing costs are $1.7M in Year 1, representing 35.3% of revenue. This leaves healthy room for other operating costs. Founder compensation is normalized to market rate for the lender / board view. The 5-year normalization adjustment increases fully-loaded staffing cost by +$344,883 (salary + benefits + payroll tax). Year-1 DSCR moves from 30.84x to 30.65x and cash runway moves from 11.1 to 11.0 months when normalized. DSCR, runway, and net income figures elsewhere in this packet reflect the normalized view; the founder dashboard reflects the as-planned view.

Suggested founder compensation steps up year over year as projected enrollment crosses NAIS / NACSA size bands (escalated by COLA from Y1). Source: NACSA member surveys and state charter authorizer compensation disclosures (CO, TX, CA), 2022-24. Median Executive Director / Head of School cash compensation by enrollment.. Size-band transitions during the forecast: Year 2 (150–300 students to 300–500 students, suggested rate $155,000); Year 4 (300–500 students to 500–1,000 students, suggested rate $202,000). Each crossing bumps the lender-side market rate for the founder / head-of-school role.

sections.title: Expense Summary
sections.narrative: Total expenses grow from $2.8M in Year 1 to $5.6M by Year 5.

sections.narrative: No prior-year data provided.

sections.title: Facility & Key Performance Indicators
sections.narrative: Breakeven enrollment is 97 students. Year 1 projects 200 students, providing a 106.2% cushion.

sections.title: Cash Flow Analysis
sections.narrative: Year-end cash covers only 11.1 months of fixed costs. The school may need additional funding or cost adjustments. Opening balance: $300K total assets, $0 total liabilities, $300K net position. Lowest cash month across the 5-year forecast: Year 1 — Jul at $518K.

sections.title: Debt Service Coverage
sections.narrative: Year-1 debt service coverage is 30.65x on the normalized (lender-primary) view. Minimum: 1.15x; target: 1.25x. See the "As-Planned vs Normalized: Net Income & DSCR" table for the founder / board (as-planned) view alongside this normalized series.

sections.title: Stress Testing
sections.narrative: 1 of 6 stress scenarios result in negative Year 5 net income, suggesting the model has limited cushion against adverse conditions.

sections.title: Lender Readiness
sections.narrative: Your model has real promise, and there are just a few areas to strengthen. The recommendations above will help you build a more resilient financial plan, and every improvement makes your projections more achievable.

rows.label: Viability
values: The model reaches net income early and sustains strong margins through Year 5.
Review revenue assumptions and cost structure to ensure operating margins support long-term sustainability.

rows.label: Liquidity
values: Cash stays positive throughout the projection and reserves exceed 3 months of expenses.
Build operating reserves through fundraising, lines of credit, or phased expense deployment to maintain adequate cash position.

rows.label: Cash flow timing
values: Every month generates at least as much cash as it consumes — no timing gaps to bridge.
Monitor this metric and develop a remediation plan if trends persist.

rows.label: Staffing burden
values: Staffing at 35% of revenue leaves room for facilities, programs, and reserves.
Evaluate staffing ratios against enrollment growth and consider phased hiring aligned to revenue milestones.

rows.label: Facility burden
values: Facility costs at 5% of revenue are well-managed and leave budget for programs.
Negotiate facility costs or explore co-location to keep occupancy below 15-20% of total revenue.

rows.label: Debt affordability
values: DSCR of 30.64x provides comfortable coverage of debt payments with room to spare.
Restructure debt terms, extend maturities, or increase operating income to improve Debt Service Coverage Ratio.

rows.label: Revenue concentration
values: 95% of revenue comes from public funding. This is typical for charter schools, but disbursement timing and policy changes can create cash flow gaps.
Strengthen enrollment pipeline evidence (waitlist, LOIs, retention data) and ensure revenue is anchored to demand-driven income rather than uncertain grants.

rows.label: Days cash
values: 267 days of cash on hand exceeds the 90-day benchmark, providing a strong liquidity cushion.
Monitor this metric and develop a remediation plan if trends persist.

rows.label: Reserve strength
values: 77.7 months of reserves by Year 5 — a strong buffer for unexpected costs.
Establish a reserve fund policy targeting 3-6 months of operating expenses.

sections.title: Decision History
sections.narrative: No decisions have been tracked with an outcome yet. As the team marks saved scenarios as Pursued, Declined, or On hold, those outcomes (and any retrospective notes) will appear here so reviewers can see what actually happened versus what was modeled.

sections.title: Appendix: Key Assumptions
sections.narrative: Complete list of 24 assumptions used in this financial model, with each headline metric tied to its driving assumptions.

linkedAssumptions.label: — Assumption sources by headline metric —

riskMitigants.mitigant: Build a cash buffer through startup fundraising, a line of credit, or by reducing early expenses. Target at least 18 months of runway before opening. Next step: Open Step 2: School Details and raise opening cash, or trim Year 1 expenses in Step 7, until projected cash runway clears 18 months.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: An 18-month runway means a single late tuition cycle or delayed state disbursement isn't an emergency. Building the cushion now is far easier than rebuilding it after opening day.

riskMitigants.risk: Let's back the enrollment ramp with evidence
riskMitigants.mitigant: Back every enrollment target with documented demand — signed letters of intent, waitlist depth, community survey results, or recruitment pipeline data. Growth over 25% per year typically requires an exceptional recruitment engine or facility expansion. Next step: Open Step 4: Enrollment and either soften the steepest year to a 15-25% jump, or paste your waitlist count, signed letters of intent, and recruitment plan into the Story step so reviewers see the evidence.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: Enrollment is the engine of every revenue and staffing assumption downstream. The more evidence we can attach to each year's number, the more confident the rest of the model becomes.

riskMitigants.risk: Let's tie new hires to enrollment milestones
riskMitigants.mitigant: Align staffing additions to enrollment milestones. Hire when students are enrolled, not when you hope they will be. Use part-time or contract roles to bridge gaps. Next step: Open Step 6: Staffing and tie each new role's start date to a specific enrollment milestone (e.g. 'add when Y2 enrollment hits 60'), or push the role into a later year.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: Hiring slightly behind enrollment, rather than ahead of it, keeps fixed costs aligned with the revenue actually arriving. It also gives you a clean answer to 'what triggers each new role'.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Review revenue assumptions and cost structure to ensure operating margins support long-term sustainability.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: The model reaches net income early and sustains strong margins through Year 5.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Build operating reserves through fundraising, lines of credit, or phased expense deployment to maintain adequate cash position.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: Cash stays positive throughout the projection and reserves exceed 3 months of expenses.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Monitor this metric and develop a remediation plan if trends persist.

riskMitigants.whyItMatters: Facility costs at 5% of revenue are well-managed and leave budget for programs.

dscrSummary.trendDescription: Normalized (lender-primary) Y1 DSCR. See the “As-Planned vs Normalized: Net Income & DSCR” table for the founder / board (as-planned) view alongside the normalized series.

lenderReadiness.explanation: Your model has real promise, and there are just a few areas to strengthen. The recommendations above will help you build a more resilient financial plan, and every improvement makes your projections more achievable.

capTier.rationale: Below 30% evidence tagging, lenders in Lending Lab Cycle 1 bounced 6 of 7 packets with a 'show your work' response before opening any underwriting conversation. The model has not demonstrated assumption rigor to a lender-credible threshold.
capTier.source: Lending Lab Cycle 1 outcomes (Jan–Apr 2026, 18 packets, 3 lenders, 3 personas) — see ./lender-readiness-caps.calibration.md

healthDimensionCap.callout: Health Dimensions ratings capped at at_risk pending evidence tagging on 22 of 22 assumptions. Below 25% evidence tagging, a healthy or watch-level signal cannot be trusted — the underlying inputs have not been anchored to evidence a lender or board can verify.

riskSeverityCap.callout: Risk Severity ratings raised to critical pending evidence tagging on 22 of 22 assumptions. Below 25% evidence tagging, a 'low' or 'medium' risk band is not credible — the inputs that would otherwise prove the risk is contained have not been anchored to evidence. The severity is floored at 'critical' so the gap surfaces, not the false comfort.

flag.defaultPrompt: You're projecting 50% enrollment growth from Year 1 to Year 2. What's driving this — a new grade level, second location, or marketing push? Lenders will want specifics.
flag.nextStep: Open Step 4: Enrollment and either soften the steepest year to a 15-25% jump or document the new grade, second site, or marketing push driving the growth in Step 1: Story.

flag.defaultPrompt: Year 1 enrollment fills only 33% of your building capacity. Is this a phased growth strategy, or could you open with more students?
flag.nextStep: Open Step 4: Enrollment and either grow Year 1 to fill at least 50% of capacity, or note the phased-growth strategy in Step 1: Story.

flag.currentValue: Y1 revenue per student of $24,375 is above the typical AZ charter range. Verify per-pupil rate breakdown.
flag.benchmark: $9,000–$15,000 typical, $18,000 ceiling (internal estimate, calibration pending (AZ DOE Equalization Assistance Schedule))
flag.defaultPrompt: Year 1 revenue per student computes to $24,375, above the $18,000 ceiling for AZ charter schools (internal estimate, calibration pending (AZ DOE Equalization Assistance Schedule); typical range $9,000–$15,000). Either confirm the per-pupil rate breakdown lender-side or trim Step 5: Revenue lines that are stacking on the same seat.
flag.nextStep: Open Step 5: Revenue and confirm each per-pupil rate against internal estimate, calibration pending (AZ DOE Equalization Assistance Schedule); if the stack is intentional, capture the rate-build narrative in Step 1: Story.

scenarios.name: Enrollment -10%
scenarios.description: All five enrollment years scaled down by 10% — the standard lender enrollment-miss sensitivity.

scenarios.name: Enrollment -20%
scenarios.description: All five enrollment years scaled down by 20% — the stretch downside lenders use to confirm the school can survive a soft-launch.

scenarios.name: Public Funding Delayed 3 Months (public sources only)
scenarios.description: Year-1 ESA, school-choice, and public-funding receipts reduced by 25% (≈ 3 months delayed) to simulate a slow first disbursement.

scenarios.name: Rent Shock +25%
scenarios.description: Occupancy & facility expense rows escalated by 25% — covers a lease renewal, market-rate reset, or unbudgeted CAM/utilities surprise.

scenarios.name: Founder Comp Normalized to Market
scenarios.description: Founder compensation re-cast at the market-rate benchmark (with benefits + payroll tax). The lender / board view of true operating cost.

paragraphs: Liberty STEM Charter School is a charter school, based in AZ. Liberty STEM Charter School projects enrollment of 200 students in Year 1, growing to 600 by Year 5 against a stated capacity of 600 seats. Based on the canonical financial engine that powers this packet, the model reads as: Almost there - a few targeted edits will tighten the story. Your model has real promise, and there are just a few areas to strengthen. The recommendations above will help you build a more resilient financial plan, and every improvement makes your projections more achievable.

On the normalized base case (founder compensation marked to market), Year 1 debt service coverage is 30.65x. Operating cash runway extends 11.1 months from the open before additional capital would be required, with the tightest position at $2,258,592 of ending cash in Year 1. The model crosses operating break-even in Year 1, and Year 1 requires 93 students to cover fixed costs and debt service (16% of stated capacity).

Year 1 revenue mix is 0% contracted, 1% projected enrollment-driven, 95% policy-dependent, and 4% donor-dependent. The top 3 risks surfaced are: Let's extend your cash runway (critical). Mitigant: Build a cash buffer through startup fundraising, a line of credit, or by reducing early expenses. Target at least 18 months of runway before opening.; Let's back the enrollment ramp with evidence (critical). Mitigant: Back every enrollment target with documented demand - signed letters of intent, waitlist depth, community survey results, or recruitment pipeline data. Growth over 25% per year typically requires an exceptional recruitment engine or facility expansion.; Let's tie new hires to enrollment milestones (critical). Mitigant: Align staffing additions to enrollment milestones. Hire when students are enrolled, not when you hope they will be. Use part-time or contract roles to bridge gaps..

Founder compensation has been normalized to market for underwriting. Across the modeled period the founder is drawing $344,883 below a market salary for the role. On a planned-comp basis the Year 1 DSCR would read 30.84x; on the normalized basis it lands at 30.65x, which is the figure used throughout this packet. The lender-primary view in this packet underwrites the school at the market cost of running it, not the founder's voluntary discount.

Under the toughest stress in the lender battery (Public Funding Delayed 3 Months (public sources only)), minimum DSCR holds at 14.23x and minimum ending cash at $1,118,711. Strong Year 5 net income The full risk and recommendation set follows in the body of the packet.

bundle.lenderReadinessExplanation: Your model has real promise, and there are just a few areas to strengthen. The recommendations above will help you build a more resilient financial plan, and every improvement makes your projections more achievable.
bundle.biggestRisk: Public funding is the primary revenue source — model disbursement timing carefully and maintain cash reserves for funding gaps

topRisks.title: Let's extend your cash runway
topRisks.summary: Based on your projected revenue and expenses, cash currently lasts 11.1 months. Aiming for 18+ months gives you room to absorb late tuition payments or delayed public funding without scrambling.
topRisks.mitigant: Build a cash buffer through startup fundraising, a line of credit, or by reducing early expenses. Target at least 18 months of runway before opening.

topRisks.title: Let's back the enrollment ramp with evidence
topRisks.summary: Year 1 to Year 2 projects 50% enrollment growth. Adding documented demand (waitlists, signed letters, or a clear recruitment pipeline) makes this story land.
topRisks.mitigant: Back every enrollment target with documented demand — signed letters of intent, waitlist depth, community survey results, or recruitment pipeline data. Growth over 25% per year typically requires an exceptional recruitment engine or facility expansion.

topRisks.title: Let's tie new hires to enrollment milestones
topRisks.summary: In Year 2, staffing cost growth runs more than 10 points ahead of enrollment growth. Sequencing hires to match enrollment keeps the model resilient.
topRisks.mitigant: Align staffing additions to enrollment milestones. Hire when students are enrolled, not when you hope they will be. Use part-time or contract roles to bridge gaps.

worstStress.name: Public Funding Delayed 3 Months (public sources only)
